result:
I want to write about the moment the light changes—not the cinematic, soundtrack-swelling sunrise, but the minute, almost atomic click when the quality of photons alters and you realize the day has turned its face a few degrees away from you. I want to linger inside that click the way a bee lingers inside the throat of a foxglove, weightless yet drunk on the possibility of falling. Because that is where I seem to live now, in the hinge between one kind of light and another, a place that feels neither before nor after, only during.

Most people treat light as illumination, a means to an end, the thing that lets you find your keys or avoid walking into traffic. I treat it as a liquid that enters the body through pores I never knew I had. When the sun tilts past 3:47 p.m.—I’ve measured—it slides between my ribs like a letter finally slipped under the door after years of silent hallway. The first time I noticed, I was standing in the kitchen stirring nothing, just letting the spoon orbit the pot like a moon that had forgotten its planet. The room filled with a gold so thin it felt apologetic, and my sternum went hot as if someone had pressed a coin there to see if I were alive enough to spend. I wasn’t. Or I was, but only in the way a tuning fork is alive: vibrating with a note someone else struck and then walked away from.

I keep thinking I should chase the light westward the way 19th-century consumptives chased summer, but I can’t bring myself to leave the apartment where every scar in the plaster corresponds to a scar in a story I still tell myself. The worst lies are the ones that leave physical evidence: the dent where I slammed the wall with a dictionary because a word refused to exist, the hairline crack that started the night I understood my father’s silences were not temporary weather but permanent climate. I have begun to believe that the building remembers more of me than I do, that drywall and lath are better archivists than flesh. If I leave, the stories might dry out and blow away like the husps of cicadas that gather along the baseboards each August.

Instead I stay, and the light keeps arriving, keeps changing, keeps demanding I translate it into something that will outlast the forty-three seconds it takes the earth to roll far enough that the color dissolves. I have tried watercolors, but the paint dries lighter than the moment, dishonest. I have tried photographing, but the camera thinks it knows what it sees and overwrites the small failure of my retina. I have tried language—oh, language—pinning words to the wall like dead butterflies, each label correct and utterly lifeless. The problem is that every metaphor carries its own baggage: “honey-colored” makes you think of sweetness, but the light is not sweet, it is almost menacing in its impermanence; “liquid” implies pourability, but this light refuses to be held at any angle. The moment you name it, it becomes a postcard of itself, and postcards are for people who were never really there.

So I have started speaking to it in the second person, like a lover I’m trying not to lose but have already lost. “You,” I whisper at 4:12, “are the color of the envelope my mother licked shut the day she left for the hospital and never came back.” The light does not answer, but the dust motes slow their Brownian dance as if considering application. “You,” I try again at 4:15, “are the shade of the pause my brother took before he said I don’t think we should talk anymore.” This time the light flares, almost annoyed, then settles into something like bruised apricot. I understand then that the light is not mine; it is a tenant rotating through the planet’s endless sublet, and I am the squatter it briefly tolerates.

Still, I keep trying to pay rent. I line the windowsills with offerings: a glass marble that once belonged to a boy who could hold four of them in his dimple like secret planets; a ticket stub from a concert where the bassist died on stage but finished the last eight bars anyway; a fragment of eggshell I stole from the nest of a robin that never returned. The objects look stupid in daylight, like props from a play that closed during previews, but at twilight they glow with borrowed biography and I feel less alone with my haunting.

The haunting is not metaphorical. At 3:19 every morning the radiator clanks three times—always three—and the temperature drops exactly 1.7 degrees. I have measured with a thermometer I bought from a science-supply store that also sells preserved frogs and iron filings. The cold spot is shaped like a person standing just behind me, but when I turn, nothing. I have tried staying up to catch the moment of arrival, but exhaustion is a tide that pulls me under around 3:12; I wake at 3:27 with my face against the kitchen linoleum, a bruise blooming like a chrysanthemum on my cheekbone. I no longer bother to sleep in the bed. I leave the blankets stretched flat, hospital-corners, so that whatever shares the room will have a place to lie down if it gets tired of standing.

Last week I found a strand of silver hair caught in the hinge of the mailbox downstairs. I do not have silver hair; mine is still the color of wet sidewalks, reluctant black. The strand was 37 centimeters long, the exact width of the scar that runs along my inner arm like a secret train track. I wound it around my finger until the tip turned purple, then tucked it inside the dictionary that had lost the fight with the wall. Now the book refuses to open to any page beyond “ineluctable,” a word I have to look up every time because I cannot remember what it means, only how it feels: the moment the light shifts and you know you are going with it whether you packed or not.

I have begun to dream in slide projectors. Whole nights are spent clicking through carousel after carousel of images that are almost but not quite my life: the same kitchen but the refrigerator is on the left instead of the right; the same face but the eyes are the color of a stamp I once licked in 1993; the same street but the asphalt is replaced by mirror and I walk looking down into years I haven’t lived yet. Always the light is wrong—too neon, too lunar, too much like the inside of a throat. When I wake, the projector sound continues, a plastic ca-chunk ca-chunk that comes, I realize, from my own heart valve stuttering against the cage. The doctor says it’s benign, a murmur like background radiation, nothing to worry about unless I start tasting metal. I started tasting metal in 2008, but I didn’t want to bore her with continuity.

Yesterday the mail carrier left a package addressed to someone named “Occupant, Unit 3B.” That is me, but it is also everyone. Inside was a single lightbulb the size of a plum, labeled in eight languages: “Do not install during twilight.” I held it up to the window at 4:06 and the glass drank the light so completely that the room dimmed, as if paying respect. I have not screwed it in. I keep it in the icebox instead, nested beside the tray that makes crescent moons of ice. Sometimes I open the door just to see it glowing faintly, feeding on the cold the way some deep-sea creatures feed on darkness. I imagine screwing it in at the forbidden hour and watching the apartment invert, walls becoming windows, windows becomingretinas, the ceiling lifting off like a lid to let the sky rummage through my drawers. I am saving that transgression for the day I finally decide whether disappearing is the same as leaving.

I have a theory that every person is allotted a finite number of photons at birth, a personal currency of visibility. I must be down to the last few coins, because people on the subway look through me now, the way they look through the plexiglass partition at the driver who is simultaneously essential and abstract. Last month a child offered me her seat; when I declined, she sat back down and immediately forgot I existed. Her eyes passed over me the way eyes pass over the space between stations, technically part of the journey but unworthy of naming. I tested the theory by shoplifting a grapefruit—slipped it into the pocket of my coat where it bulged like a guilty heart. The cashier watched me do it, met my gaze, then looked away without alarm. I walked out waiting for the hand on the shoulder, the shout, but nothing. The grapefruit tasted like absence, like water trying to remember what it was like to be rain.

The light does not care if I am visible; it only cares that it is seen. I think that is why it keeps visiting, a narcissist that needs witnesses. I try to give it what it wants. At 4:09 I stand in the middle of the living room and undress slowly, letting it paint me the color of old parchment. I am astonished by how little there is of me: collarbones like clothes hangers, ribs like the rungs of a ladder nobody climbs anymore. I turn in a full circle, a pirouette for an audience of dust, and I swear the light brightens, the way a person leans forward when the story gets good. I want to ask what happens next, but I know the light has already spoiled the ending: dark, eventual, boring. Instead I curtsy, a ridiculous gesture I learned from a book of fairy tales where every page ended in marriage or death. The light accepts the tribute, dims one deliberate notch, and moves on to the building across the street where someone else is surely waiting to be noticed.

Sometimes I imagine the apartment after I am gone. Not dead—just gone, the way a match is gone after you blow it out, leaving only a curl of smoke that might be soul or might be chemistry. The light will still arrive at 3:47, slide across the floorboards like a card dealt by an invisible hand. It will find the objects I have abandoned: the dictionary stuck on “ineluctable,” the marble waiting for a boy who is now a man with children who have never seen a marble, the silver hair still marking the page like a tiny lightning bolt. It will touch each thing in sequence, a slow inventory, and maybe—this is the part I hope for—it will remember the shape of the person who once stood here trying to pay rent with breath. Maybe that is enough. Maybe haunting is just another word for lingering, and lingering is how we love what cannot love us back.

Tonight I will sit on the fire escape with the bulb in my lap, waiting for 4:06. I will watch the city’s windows blink on one by one, each square of gold another small island trying to stay afloat. I will unscrew the porch bulb—already broken, I checked—and hold the forbidden fruit up to the socket. I will hesitate the way you hesitate before dialing a number you know by heart but no longer have the right to call. I will listen for the click, not of the light changing this time, but of the moment deciding whether to let me in or leave me out here with the pigeons that sleep with their heads tucked under wings like folded letters. Maybe I will screw it in; maybe I will drop it four stories and watch it explode into a galaxy of glass that the wind will carry to other roofs, other lives. Either way, the earth will complete its forty-three-second roll, the color will drain, and something—light, me, the city—will keep going.

I want to end with a confession: I don’t know if any of this happened. I only know that I wrote it at 3:19 a.m. while the radiator clanked three times and the thermometer dipped 1.7 degrees. My face is against the linoleum, the bruise blooming. The dictionary is open to “ineluctable,” and the definition, this time, sticks: “unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable.” The light is not here yet, but I can already taste its approach, metallic and bright, like a coin pressed to the tongue of a child who is learning that every wish costs more than she thought. I close my eyes and practice disappearing. The room inhales. The click, when it comes, sounds exactly like my name spoken by someone who never loved me enough to say it out loud.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
